children.name: Coping and Efficacy
children.description: In the context of the psychological perspective, particularly within Protection Motivation Theory (PMT), coping appraisal determines behavior through *response efficacy*—the belief that a recommended protective action will effectively mitigate a threat—and *self-efficacy*—the individual's confidence in their own capability to execute that action (Hanus & Wu, 2016, p. 3; Sulaiman et al., 2022, p. 3). When individuals perceive high levels of both efficacies, they are motivated to adopt adaptive coping behaviors, such as compliance with security policies, whereas low efficacy coupled with high threat perception often leads to maladaptive responses like avoidance or denial (Pham, 2017; Thangavelu et al., 2021, p. 5). Consequently, security education initiatives must aim to bolster these efficacy beliefs to foster resilience and encourage proactive security behaviors rather than helplessness (Baral & Arachchilage, 2019, p. 5; Burns et al., 2015, p. 3933).

children.name: Self-Efficacy
children.description: Self-efficacy is defined as an individual's judgment of their capability to execute specific actions to thwart cyber threats, acting as a vital component of coping appraisal that influences motivation and persistence in security tasks (Baral & Arachchilage, 2019, p. 4; Thangavelu et al., 2021, p. 5). Research indicates that increased security awareness, particularly regarding countermeasures and procedural knowledge, positively enhances self-efficacy, thereby empowering users to confidently implement protective behaviors (Hanus & Wu, 2016; Baral & Arachchilage, 2019, p. 5). Conversely, without high self-efficacy, users may fail to adopt necessary security measures or technologies even if they recognize the severity of a threat, as they lack the confidence to perform the required coping actions (Hanus & Wu, 2016; reeves et al., 2021, p. 8).

children.name: Response Efficacy
children.description: Response efficacy constitutes a fundamental element of the coping appraisal process within Protection Motivation Theory (PMT), serving as the user's belief—or "outcome expectation"—that a specific recommended countermeasure will effectively mitigate a targeted security threat (Hanus & Wu, 2016, p. 3; Pham, 2017, p. 3). Within this behavioral architecture, response efficacy functions interdependently with self-efficacy and response cost to determine whether an individual adopts adaptive protective behaviors or falls into maladaptive non-compliance (Ali et al., 2021, p. 11; Hanus & Wu, 2016, p. 4). Furthermore, expanded frameworks like the Technology Threat Avoidance Theory (TTAT) structure coping efficacy into external appraisals, regarding the effectiveness of protective technology, and internal appraisals, regarding the individual's own ability to detect and avoid threats (reeves et al., 2021, p. 8).

children.name: Personal Agency
children.description: Based on the psychological perspectives of cybersecurity, the architecture of personal agency is described as a critical mental construct that, along with attitude and perceived norms, directly forms an individual's behavioral intention to comply with security measures. This framework relies heavily on coping appraisal mechanisms, specifically self-efficacy (the belief in one's ability to execute security tasks) and response efficacy (the belief that the action will effectively mitigate the threat), which determine motivation. Ultimately, a robust sense of personal agency enables users to mobilize cognitive resources and persist in protective behaviors when coping with complex cyber threats.

children.name: Behavioural Drivers
children.description: Behavioural drivers are the psychological antecedents—including attitudes, subjective norms, and self-efficacy—that determine an individual's intention to engage in specific security actions, often analyzed through frameworks like the Theory of Planned Behavior (Bulgurcu et al., 2010, p. 528). These drivers encompass rational assessments of the costs and benefits associated with compliance, as well as cognitive appraisals regarding the severity of threats and the individual's ability to cope with them (Bulgurcu et al., 2010, p. 529; Sulaiman et al., 2022, p. 3). Conversely, specific behavioural drivers for non-compliance include psychological factors such as security-related stress, neutralization techniques, and value conflicts (Asfoor et al., 2022, p. 278).

children.name: Intrinsic motivation
children.description: Intrinsic motivation in cybersecurity is defined as the internal drive to engage in secure behaviors for their own sake, characterized by feelings of enjoyment, satisfaction, and personal accomplishment rather than the pursuit of external rewards (Ali et al., 2021, p. 27; Bada et al., 2019, p. 7). Within this context, behavioral drivers stem from an individual's personal values and beliefs, such as perceived legitimacy and value congruence, which research indicates are often more significant predictors of policy compliance than extrinsic factors (Ali et al., 2021, p. 10). Furthermore, intrinsic benefits—such as self-esteem and the justification of actions through internal inspiration—help employees internalize security rules, leading to more sustainable behavioral changes (Bulgurcu et al., 2010, p. 530). **References** * Ali, R. F., Dominic, P. D. D., Ali, S. E. A., Rehman, M., & Sohail, M. (2021). Information security behavior and information security policy compliance: A systematic literature review for identifying the transformation process from noncompliance to compliance. *Applied Sciences*, *11*(8), 3383. * Bada, M., Sasse, A. M., & Nurse, J. R. (2019). Cyber security awareness campaigns: Why do they fail to change behaviour?. *arXiv preprint arXiv:1901.02672*. * Bulgurcu, B., Cavusoglu, H., & Benbasat, I. (2010). Information security policy compliance: An empirical study of rationality-based beliefs and information security awareness. *MIS Quarterly*, *34*(3), 523–548.

children.name: Moral beliefs
children.description: Moral beliefs in the context of cybersecurity behavior refer to an individual's intrinsic evaluation of whether a specific action, such as violating an information security policy (ISP) is ethically right or wrong (Ali et al., 2021, p. 22). These beliefs function as an overriding internal control mechanism that can supersede other motivations, such as utility maximization or convenience, often proving to be a stronger predictor of compliance than external deterrents like sanctions (Vance et al., 2020). Empirical research demonstrates that moral beliefs consistently exert a strong negative influence on the intention to violate security policies, a finding that holds true across diverse national cultures (Vance et al., 2020). **Sources:** * Ali, R. F., et al. (2021). Information Security Behavior and Information Security Policy Compliance: A Systematic Literature Review for Identifying the Transformation Process from Noncompliance to Compliance. *Applied Sciences, 11*(3383) 22. * Vance, A., Siponen, M. T., & Straub, D. W. (2020). Effects of sanctions, moral beliefs, and neutralization on information security policy violations across cultures. *Information & Management, 57*(4) Article 103212.

children.name: Counter-neutralization
children.description: Counter-neutralization, often described as anti-neutralization, involves deploying communication strategies that proactively refute the specific rationalizations and excuses—such as denial of responsibility or injury—that employees use to justify non-compliant information security behaviors (Alyami et al., 2023, p. 69; Hu et al., 2021, p. 11). By challenging these psychological justifications, organizations aim to prevent individuals from alleviating the moral guilt associated with violating security policies, thereby maintaining the psychological barrier against misconduct (Vance et al., 2020, p. 3). Ultimately, this intervention seeks to block the cognitive process of neutralization to improve adherence to information security policies (reeves et al., 2021, p. 18).

children.name: Cognitive Barriers
children.description: Cognitive barriers in cybersecurity stem from the limitations of human information processing, where individuals often rely on intuitive, heuristic-based thinking (System 1) rather than analytic reasoning (System 2), making them susceptible to biases that distort risk perception and decision-making (Skinner et al., 2018, p. 4; Tsohou et al., 2015, p. 131). These cognitive biases, such as the optimism bias or the availability heuristic, act as filters that prevent the proper internalization of security policies, causing users to underestimate threats even when they possess the necessary theoretical knowledge (Tariq et al., 2014, p. 2; Tsohou et al., 2015, p. 132). Furthermore, cognitive barriers encompass the depletion of mental resources, manifested as cognitive fatigue or habituation, which limits an individual's capacity to process security warnings and leads to disengagement from security-related behaviors (reeves et al., 2021, p. 5).

children.name: Security Fatigue
children.description:
Security fatigue describes a state of weariness or aversion where users feel overwhelmed by the repetitive demands, warnings, and advice required to maintain cybersecurity. This leads to a depletion of the cognitive resources necessary for decision-making.

This cognitive barrier often prompts individuals to perform a subconscious cost-benefit analysis—sometimes referred to as a **"compliance budget"**—where they conclude that the effort to comply outweighs the perceived risk or benefit.

Consequently, this fatigue results in disengagement and non-compliant behaviors, such as ignoring security alerts or rejecting good practices, as users struggle to cope with the cognitive burden of constant security vigilance.
children.tldr: Users become overwhelmed and apathetic when bombarded with too many security warnings and requirements.
children.examples:
- An employee ignoring a valid phishing warning after receiving 10 false positive alerts that week.
- Re-using the same simple password across multiple systems because the password rotation policy requires a change every 30 days.
actionItems: Implement Single Sign-On (SSO) to reduce password fatigue.
Consolidate security alerts so users only see high-priority, actionable warnings.
Shift from annual, hours-long training to 3-minute, highly relevant monthly micro-learnings.
kpis: % of users ignoring or bypassing security prompts
Password reset request volume over time
Drop-off rate in voluntary security training modules

maturityLevels.level1: Constant interruptions. Policies demand complex passwords rotated frequently. Users frequently bypass controls.
maturityLevels.level5: Frictionless security. SSO and passwordless authentication are standard. Alerts are rare, highly accurate, and actionable.

children.name: Optimism Barriers
children.description: Optimism bias is a cognitive barrier where individuals consistently believe they are less likely to experience negative events, such as cyberattacks, compared to others, leading them to overestimate their own control over security threats (Tsohou et al., 2015, p. 10; Tsohou, 2015, p. 18). This psychological tendency causes users to assign higher probabilities to positive outcomes and discount the likelihood of personal risk, often resulting in the dismissal of evidence regarding their own vulnerability (Tariq et al., 2014, p. 3; Moustafa et al., 2021, p. 6). Consequently, individuals may fail to adopt necessary protective behaviors because they assume that while security threats exist, they will not personally fall victim to them (Tsohou, 2015, p. 12).

children.name: Habituation
children.description: Habituation in cybersecurity is a cognitive process where a user’s response to repeated stimuli, such as security warnings or policy reminders, diminishes over time due to frequent and predictable exposure (Moustafa et al., 2021, p. 7; reeves et al., 2021, p. 6). This phenomenon acts as a cognitive barrier because individuals unconsciously filter out these cues, often dismissing warnings without processing the content or consciously assessing the risk (Jager & Eckhardt, 2021, p. 20; reeves et al., 2021, p. 6). Consequently, habituated users may engage in insecure behaviors, such as ignoring alerts, not due to a lack of knowledge but because the repetitive nature of the stimuli fails to trigger active decision-making (reeves et al., 2021, p. 11).

children.name: Cognitive Biases
children.description: Cognitive biases act as mental shortcuts and psychological filters that influence how individuals process security-related information, often leading to intuitive or experiential reasoning rather than rational risk assessment (Tsohou et al., 2015, p. 2). These barriers, which include specific heuristics such as optimism bias or anchoring, can cause users to underestimate vulnerabilities or rely on automatic, effortless decision-making that disregards security protocols (reeves et al., 2021, p. 7; Skinner et al., 2018, p. XX:4). Consequently, traditional awareness programs that rely solely on the normative transfer of knowledge often fail to change behavior because they do not account for the distorting effect these biases have on an individual's internalization of security policies (Tsohou et al., 2015, p. 3).

children.name: Delivery Frequency
children.description: From a design perspective, delivery frequency refers to the strategic scheduling of SETA interventions, where scholars advocate for continuous, periodic training cycles rather than singular annual events to foster habit formation and knowledge retention (Hu et al., 2021, p. 5; Yeoh et al., 2021, p. 11). This approach requires balancing the necessity of keeping security top-of-mind against the risk of inducing security fatigue through excessive repetition or information overload (Alshaikh et al., 2021, p. 12; He & Zhang, 2019, p. 256). Consequently, effective design prioritizes frequent, short educational moments or "just-in-time" training over infrequent, intensive sessions to better influence long-term behavioral change (Crumbaugh, 2019, p. 6; Taherdoost, 2024, p. 1654).

children.name: Just In Time Training
children.description:
Timing is one of the most underappreciated variables in security awareness training. Just-in-Time (JIT) training is built around a simple insight: rather than scheduling education in advance and hoping it transfers to real behaviour later, JIT triggers training at the exact moment a mistake occurs (Yeoh et al., 2021; Oldham & McAlpine, 2019).
When an employee clicks a simulated phishing link and is immediately shown what they missed, they are processing that information in the most receptive state possible. The mistake is fresh, the context is live, and the lesson is directly relevant. He and Zhang (2019) put it plainly: educate at the exact moment a mistake is realised, not days later in a follow-up module (p. 253). This approach reduces repeat errors and builds genuine empathy with the consequences of a security failure in a way that abstract training rarely achieves (Yeoh et al., 2021; Oldham & McAlpine, 2019).

The contrast with annual compliance training is stark. Front-loading information once a year, relying on rote memorisation, and assuming it will remain accessible months later simply does not work (Alshaikh et al., 2021; Yeoh et al., 2021). JIT works because it integrates correction into the moment of failure rather than treating training as a separate event.

For phishing specifically, habit formation requires repetition. Yeoh et al. (2021) recommend at least six JIT cycles spaced roughly 28 days apart to build sustained alertness rather than a short-term improvement that fades.

The risk to manage is over-exposure. Interventions that are too frequent or predictable tip into habituation, where employees tune out entirely (Reeves et al., 2021, p. 6). Varying format and maintaining realistic conditions are the practical levers for keeping JIT effective over time.
children.tldr: Deliver security training at the exact moment a mistake occurs, capitalizing on high learner receptivity to correct behavior in real-time.
children.examples:
- An employee clicking a simulated phishing link and immediately seeing a landing page explaining the specific clues they missed.
- A user receiving an instant warning alert and brief safety guidance when trying to paste a password into an insecure text field.
actionItems: Integrate automated training triggers with phishing simulation platforms to launch JIT landing pages.
Design JIT learning sequences to run for at least six cycles spaced 28 days apart to form lasting habits.
Rotate simulated scenarios and templates frequently to prevent employee habituation.
kpis: Repeat failure rate on phishing simulations
Average response time of user engagement with JIT feedback
Reduction in click-through rate over six JIT cycles

children.name: Continuous Reinforcement
children.description:
A single training event, however well designed, will not produce lasting behaviour change. Continuous reinforcement is the recognition that security awareness is a long-term investment requiring ongoing engagement rather than periodic compliance exercises (Sabillon et al., 2019, p. 26). The shift is from intensive "binge training" to frequent, short interventions woven into the rhythm of daily work (Oldham & McAlpine, 2019, p. 104).

The case for this approach is partly about the forgetting curve. Without regular reinforcement, knowledge decays quickly and the gains from even effective training erode over time. A continuous model with a deliberate reinforcement phase directly counters this, keeping security behaviours active rather than dormant between annual sessions (Sabillon et al., 2019). Micro-learning is the practical mechanism: short, focused interventions that maintain engagement without demanding significant time or attention (He & Zhang, 2019; Taherdoost, 2024). JIT nudges sit alongside this, triggering contextual reinforcement at the moment a risky behaviour occurs, such as attempting to share a confidential file, rather than waiting for the next scheduled session (Nurse et al., 2025, p. 99). Together, these approaches move security awareness beyond a compliance checkbox toward something closer to a culture of ongoing vigilance (Alshaikh et al., 2019; Thamae, 2024).

The critical caveat is frequency. There is a tipping point beyond which continuous reinforcement becomes counterproductive. Overly frequent or predictable interventions cause habituation, employees begin filtering them out unconsciously, and the signal is lost in the noise (Reeves et al., 2021, p. 6). Push further and the cognitive load of constant security decisions produces fatigue and, eventually, reactance, where employees deliberately disengage to reclaim a sense of autonomy over their own work (Reeves et al., 2021, p. 9; He & Zhang, 2019, p. 257).

The practical target is regularity without predictability, and volume without overwhelm. Varying the format, spacing interventions thoughtfully, and monitoring for signs of disengagement are the levers practitioners have for keeping continuous reinforcement on the right side of that line.
children.tldr: Shift from annual training to frequent, short learning moments that build lasting habits and combat knowledge decay.
children.examples:
- Delivering a 2-minute monthly micro-learning module instead of a single annual 60-minute training session.
- Utilizing automated, context-specific nudges when an employee attempts to send an unencrypted sensitive file.
actionItems: Break down existing annual training modules into brief, monthly micro-learning sessions.
Establish a varied delivery schedule with mixed formats (videos, quick quizzes, nudges) to avoid fatigue.
Deploy automated context-specific nudges at the point of action for high-risk behaviors.
kpis: Long-term security knowledge retention rate over a 12-month period
Monthly participation and completion rate of micro-learning modules
Frequency of insecure behaviors (e.g., sharing unencrypted files) pre- and post-nudging

children.name: Drip-Feed Learning
children.description:
The premise of drip-feed learning is straightforward: a little, often, beats a lot, rarely. Rather than delivering security training in large periodic blocks, the drip-feed model breaks content into very short stimulus packages, brief videos, quick quizzes, behavioural nudges, distributed consistently over time (Skinner et al., 2018). Taherdoost (2024) frames this as micro-learning, dividing information into bite-sized units that allow self-paced, focused engagement without overwhelming the learner.
The contrast with traditional delivery is sharp. Reeves et al. (2021) liken annual full-day training sessions to cramming for an exam: information gets loaded into short-term memory, but fades quickly once the session ends (p. 13). The same total volume of content, redistributed as 30 two-minute sessions across a year, keeps the topic consistently present rather than intensely visible once and then forgotten. That sustained presence is what builds the kind of top-of-mind awareness that actually influences behaviour in the moment a threat appears (Reeves et al., 2021, p. 13).
The habit formation argument is where drip-feed learning makes its strongest case. Behavioural change does not come from a single exposure, however well designed. It comes from repetition spaced over time, reinforcing the same principles until the response becomes instinctive rather than deliberate. Skinner et al. (2018) link this directly to return on investment: intermittent learning produces more permanent behavioural change than massed training, making it not just a pedagogical preference but a practical business case (p. 7). Regular micro-trainings and communications keep security woven into daily working life rather than cordoned off as an annual compliance event (He & Zhang, 2019, p. 253).

For practitioners, the implementation shift is less about content and more about cadence. The question is not what to teach, but how to keep it present.
children.tldr: Deliver training in small, spaced micro-units to build top-of-mind awareness and create permanent security habits.
children.examples:
- Distributing thirty 2-minute interactive video lessons over a year instead of a single 60-minute training session.
- Pushing a quick 1-question security challenge to employee chat applications once a week.
actionItems: Deconstruct long-form training modules into bite-sized stimulus packages under 3 minutes.
Deploy a spaced repetition schedule to reinforce key cybersecurity principles repeatedly.
Establish a micro-learning distribution channel through internal chat platforms or email digests.
kpis: Average monthly training engagement rate
Retention of security concepts tested 3 and 6 months post-exposure
Daily active participation in optional quick-quiz challenges

children.name: Communications Strategy
children.description:
Security communication has a frequency problem and a relevance problem, and they need to be solved together. Sending more emails about cybersecurity does not build awareness if employees have learned to ignore them. The shift the literature points to is away from sporadic, compliance-driven notifications toward continuous, multi-channel engagement that treats internal security communication the way a marketer would treat a brand campaign (Alshaikh et al., 2021; Prümmer et al., 2024).

The frequency case is well established. Knowledge fades without reinforcement, and e-learning gains in particular tend to erode within six months without regular refreshers (Rantos et al., 2012, p. 264). Micro-trainings at regular intervals, supported by newsletters, blogs, and email communications, keep security consistently present rather than visible once a year and then forgotten (He & Zhang, 2019, p. 253). Oldham and McAlpine (2019) suggest a practical structure: focus the entire organisation on one specific topic per month, backed by social forum discussions and consistent messaging across channels (p. 114). This drip-feed rhythm allows employees to absorb information at their own pace while staying aligned with the programme's priorities (Oldham & McAlpine, 2019, p. 109).

Variety is what makes high-frequency communication sustainable. A method mix, drawing on newsletters, posters, screensavers, workshops, floor stickers, and web banners, prevents any single format from becoming invisible through repetition (Alshaikh et al., 2021, p. 7; Rantos et al., 2012, p. 261). Consistent design and tone across all of these touchpoints matters more than it might seem: visual and tonal coherence builds subconscious recognition, so employees register security messaging even when they are not actively engaging with it (Oldham & McAlpine, 2019). Treating the programme like an internal brand, with a name, a visual identity, and campaign materials, significantly improves recognition and recall (Alshaikh et al., 2021; Prümmer et al., 2024).

Relevance is the other half of the equation. Frequency without relevance produces noise. Content that connects to employees' personal lives, their families, their home security, their own accounts, earns attention in a way that corporate policy framing does not (He & Zhang, 2019, p. 253; Oldham & McAlpine, 2019, p. 111). Communication style also needs to flex for cultural context. Employees who operate within hierarchical cultures tend to respond better to authoritative, top-down messaging, while those from more egalitarian backgrounds are more receptive to participatory, peer-level communication (Tsohou et al., 2015, p. 15). Getting the channel right matters less than getting the framing right for the audience receiving it.
children.tldr: Run multi-channel security communication like an internal marketing campaign, combining frequency, format variety, and personal relevance to drive recognition.
children.examples:
- Designing a unified visual identity and name (e.g., 'Security Heroes') for all internal awareness materials.
- Running a monthly theme (e.g., 'Phishing Awareness') across emails, digital signs, and intranet banners.
- Adapting training messaging from authoritative directives for hierarchical teams to collaborative tips for egalitarian ones.
actionItems: Establish a cohesive internal brand and design guidelines for all cybersecurity communications.
Create a monthly theme calendar supported by multi-channel assets (digital signage, blogs, newsletters).
Segment communications to include personal safety relevance (e.g., securing home networks or personal email).
kpis: Recall and recognition rate of security campaign messages in surveys
E-mail open rates and link click-through rates for security announcements
Employee adoption rate of personal security tips (e.g. download rate of recommended home antivirus tools)

children.name: Engagement Methods
children.description: From a design perspective, engagement methods transform cybersecurity training from passive compliance tasks into interactive experiences by prioritizing attributes such as fun, personalization, and hands-on simulation to motivate employees (Alnajim et al., 2023, p. 2; He & Zhang, 2019, p. 28). These methods frequently employ gamification, utilizing mechanics like leaderboards and rewards to enhance user participation and emotional investment in the material (Barendse, 2023, p. 14; Oldham & McAlpine, 2019, p. 109). Furthermore, effective engagement design leverages marketing techniques and storytelling to ensure security concepts are relatable and memorable, thereby fostering a stronger organizational security culture (Oldham & McAlpine, 2019, p. 106).

children.name: Gamification
children.description:
Gamification is one of the most practical tools available for tackling a problem every security team knows well: employees who are disengaged, fatigued, or simply tuned out. By applying game mechanics, points, leaderboards, storytelling, and competition, to security training, organisations can shift the experience from something people endure to something they actually engage with (Scholefield & Shepherd, 2019, p. 2; Barendse, 2023, p. 7).
The motivational case is straightforward. Security consistently gets deprioritised under day-to-day work pressure, so training needs to earn attention rather than demand it. Mechanics like leaderboards tap into natural human drivers, status, competition, the fear of falling behind peers, to create genuine engagement (Oldham & McAlpine, 2019, p. 119). For global organisations, gamification has the added advantage of working across cultural boundaries; the language of play tends to translate well (Barendse, 2023, p. 55).
Beyond motivation, the learning benefits are significant. Traditional training puts employees in a passive role, reading policies, watching slides, ticking boxes. Gamification flips this by placing users in realistic scenarios where they have to make decisions, not just absorb information. The critical difference is immediate feedback: when someone makes a wrong call in a game, they find out instantly and learn from it, rather than waiting for an annual test result (Barendse, 2023, p. 55; Yeoh et al., 2021, p. 6). This also builds self-efficacy, the confidence to recognise and respond to real threats, which is something passive formats struggle to achieve (Baral & Arachchilage, 2019, p. 1).
That said, practitioners should be clear-eyed about where gamification fits. It works best as part of a broader programme, alongside phishing simulations, videos, and communications campaigns, rather than as a standalone solution (Barendse, 2023, p. 32; Alshaikh et al., 2021, p. 13). A one-off gamified module will not produce lasting behaviour change; it needs to be embedded in a continuous cycle of reinforcement (Barendse, 2023, p. 83). Quality also matters enormously here. Slapping a progress bar onto a multiple-choice quiz is not gamification in any meaningful sense. The real value comes from well-designed, scenario-based experiences, think escape rooms or branching simulations, that genuinely replicate the decisions employees face (Barendse, 2023, p. 61).
There are real limitations to acknowledge. Building high-quality gamified content takes time and money, more so than standard e-learning (Barendse, 2023, p. 110). It also does not land equally with all audiences. Senior leaders and older employees sometimes find game formats patronising or irrelevant, and pushing gamification on a resistant audience can do more harm than good (Barendse, 2023, p. 53; Scholefield & Shepherd, 2019, p. 5). Finally, the evidence base has gaps: there is limited long-term research confirming that the behaviour changes gamification produces actually stick over time (Hendrix et al., 2016, p. 58; Scholefield & Shepherd, 2019, p. 7). Used thoughtfully, it is a powerful engagement tool, but it is not a silver bullet.
children.tldr: Apply game mechanics and branching scenarios to drive active learning, build self-efficacy, and increase engagement among fatigued employees.
children.examples:
- Designing an interactive 'security escape room' challenge where teams solve puzzles related to physical security and tailgating.
- Implementing a departmental leaderboard showing scores from simulated phishing catches and training module completions.
actionItems: Select and implement game mechanics (points, badges, leaderboards) within the existing learning management system.
Replace passive text quizzes with interactive, branching scenarios or decision-based mini-games.
Conduct target audience segmentation to tailor game elements to different demographics and ensure buy-in from senior leadership.
kpis: Active participation and voluntary completion rates of security training modules
User sentiment and satisfaction ratings regarding training format
Pre- and post-test self-efficacy scores evaluating user confidence in threat detection

children.name: Hands-On Simulations
children.description:
If gamification is about making security training engaging, hands-on simulations are about making it real. The core idea is simple: people learn better by doing than by watching or reading. Simulations close the gap between knowing what to do and actually doing it, by placing employees in realistic scenarios where they have to apply security thinking under conditions that mirror actual threats (Alnajim et al., 2023, p. 6; Wen et al., 2019, p. 2).

The reason traditional methods fall short is not just that they are boring. It is that they separate learning from context. A policy document tells you what phishing is; a simulated phishing email makes you experience it. This "situated learning" approach, where training replicates the environment in which threats actually occur, is what makes simulations effective at transferring knowledge into daily behaviour (Wen et al., 2019, p. 2; Mirkovic et al., 2015, p. 26). The other key ingredient is immediate feedback. When an employee clicks a simulated malicious link and is instantly shown what they missed, the lesson lands in a way that a post-course debrief never could (Yeoh et al., 2021, p. 6; Oldham & McAlpine, 2019, p. 119).

Simulations come in several forms, and the right choice depends on your audience. Phishing campaigns are the most accessible and widely used, giving organisations a live read on workforce vulnerability while simultaneously delivering training to those who need it most (Vasileiou & Furnell, 2019, p. 13; Yeoh et al., 2021, p. 7). For technical teams, cyber ranges and wargames, including Capture the Flag exercises, offer a safe environment to practise defence and incident response without putting real systems at risk (Alnajim et al., 2023, p. 7; Sabillon, 2021, p. 172). At the higher end, VR environments can achieve retention rates of around 80% for security principles, significantly outperforming standard methods, though the investment required is substantial (Alnajim et al., 2023, p. 12). Role-playing scenarios occupy a useful middle ground, assigning employees realistic roles and exposing them to the time pressure and competing priorities that make real threats so easy to miss (Wen et al., 2019, p. 3).
The evidence broadly supports simulations as superior to passive methods for improving threat detection (Wen et al., 2019, p. 9; Abawajy, 2014, p. 245). However, practitioners should watch for a few pitfalls. One counterintuitive finding showed that employees who received awareness training were actually more likely to fall for a phishing simulation than untrained peers, possibly due to overconfidence or simply being more active online (Back & Guerette, 2021, p. 440, 443). Habituation is another real risk: if simulations become predictable, employees stop treating them as genuine events and the learning value collapses (Reeves et al., 2021, p. 6). Varying the format, timing, and complexity of simulations is essential to keeping them effective over time.
children.tldr: Bridge the gap between theoretical knowledge and practical behavior through realistic, hands-on simulations that deliver immediate feedback.
children.examples:
- Simulating realistic, targeted phishing emails to measure and train employee vulnerability in real-time.
- Organizing a technical Capture the Flag (CTF) incident response simulation for the IT security team.
- Immersing employees in a VR workspace simulation to identify physical security threats.
actionItems: Deploy regular, varied phishing simulations to general staff with JIT feedback loops.
Set up role-playing scenario workshops for business units, incorporating time-pressure elements.
Vary simulation templates, sender addresses, and timings to prevent user habituation.
kpis: Phishing simulation click-through rate and reporting rate
Mean Time to Detect (MTTD) during simulation exercises
Reduction in repeat-fail rates across simulation cycles

children.name: Storytelling
children.description:
Security awareness often fails not because employees lack information, but because the information never connects. Policies get read and forgotten. Compliance modules get clicked through. Storytelling addresses this at the root: it turns abstract security concepts into human experiences that people can actually relate to, remember, and act on (Oldham & McAlpine, 2019, p. 116).

The mechanism is straightforward. When someone hears a story about a colleague who got a convincing email from what looked like their CEO, they do not just learn a fact about phishing. They feel the plausibility of it. That emotional connection is what drives behaviour change, not the policy that says "verify unusual requests." He and Zhang (2019) put it simply: sharing real experiences makes security material personal, and personal material sticks (p. 251). The goal is to lead learners through a narrative that links to their own background and daily context, so that complex concepts feel grounded rather than abstract (Oldham & McAlpine, 2019, p. 118).

This is where storytelling becomes especially powerful as a design tool for gamification and simulation. A list of dos and don'ts is easy to ignore. A character navigating a realistic series of security dilemmas, encountering Wi-Fi risks while working remotely, then a suspicious password reset request, then a social engineering call, creates a coherent experience that flows logically from one risk to the next (Barendse, 2023, p. 50, 51). Employees see themselves in those situations. When they return to their actual inboxes, the memory of the character's mistake is more accessible than any bullet point from a training module (Barendse, 2023, p. 50). Placing even a simple quiz within an overarching storyline measurably increases immersion and engagement (Scholefield & Shepherd, 2019, p. 7).

Video drama takes this further by adding emotional weight. Training built around a recognisable peer, someone who looks and works like the audience, increases self-efficacy: employees are more likely to adopt secure behaviours when they see someone like themselves modelling them (Skinner et al., 2018, p. 4). Crucially, emotion is not just a nice-to-have here. Skinner et al. (2018) argue that it is a fundamental component of rational decision-making, and that video drama can provoke the kind of emotional response that purely text-based training simply cannot (p. 5). Emerging formats like VR-based storytelling are extending this further still, with early evidence suggesting stronger awareness outcomes than traditional e-learning (Alnajim et al., 2023, p. 12).

For practitioners, the takeaway is this: the format of your content matters less than whether it gives people something to connect with. Characters, conflict, consequence, and context are not creative luxuries. They are the delivery mechanism for the lessons you actually want people to retain.
children.tldr: Use narrative structures, relatable characters, and emotional conflict to make security policies memorable and drive behavior change.
children.examples:
- Creating a video drama series following a fictional employee who falls for a business email compromise (BEC) scam.
- Sharing anonymous, real-world case studies of security incidents that occurred within the company in the monthly newsletter.
- Framing a remote work training module around a day-in-the-life narrative of a traveling sales representative.
actionItems: Integrate character-driven narrative arcs into branching scenario designs.
Produce short, high-production-value video dramas featuring realistic workplace security dilemmas.
Publish internal case studies of real or simulated security incidents to provide organizational context.
kpis: Employee retention of key training takeaways 6 months post-training
Qualitative feedback score on training engagement and relatability
Self-reported changes in security behavior inspired by specific training stories

children.name: Interactive Media
children.description:
Interactive media is the broader category that ties together many of the engagement methods already discussed. Serious games, simulations, VR environments, branching scenarios, and even dynamic warning systems all fall under this umbrella. What they share is a common purpose: moving employees from passive recipients of security information to active participants in their own learning (Wen et al., 2019, p. 2; Alnajim et al., 2023, p. 5).

The case against passive methods is well established at this point. Lectures, newsletters, and policy documents are not ineffective because they are poorly written. They are ineffective because they ask nothing of the person receiving them. There is no decision to make, no consequence to experience, no feedback to process. Interactive media changes this by requiring employees to engage, analyse, and respond, which is precisely what develops practical skill rather than surface-level awareness (Mirkovic et al., 2015, p. 26). Research backs this up: employees not only prefer interactive formats over text-based ones, they identify threats faster as a result of them (Abawajy, 2014, as cited in Hu et al., 2021, p. 5).

One important nuance for practitioners is that interactivity exists on a spectrum, and more is not always better. Extremely rich or complex media can overload users cognitively, making it harder rather than easier to acquire skills if the interface itself becomes an obstacle (Hu et al., 2021, p. 5). The goal is meaningful interaction, not interaction for its own sake. A well-designed branching scenario with clear choices and immediate feedback will outperform a technically impressive VR environment that distracts from the learning objective.

The limitations here are worth taking seriously. High-fidelity interactive content, custom games, VR simulations, and cyber ranges, demands significant investment in time and budget that many organisations cannot easily justify (Alnajim et al., 2023, p. 7; Barendse, 2023, p. 110). There is also a subtler risk on the other end of the spectrum. Certain interactive formats, particularly polymorphic warnings that constantly demand user decisions, can tip from engagement into fatigue. When employees feel bombarded by required interactions, the result can be "reactance," a psychological pushback where people start deliberately ignoring security prompts in order to reclaim a sense of autonomy over their own work (Reeves et al., 2021, p. 11). This is a useful reminder that the objective of interactive media is to build security habits, not to create friction.
Used well, interactive media is among the most effective tools available for security awareness. The practical challenge is matching the format to the audience, the resource envelope, and the specific behaviour you are trying to change.
children.tldr: Implement interactive learning formats (serious games, simulations, branching scenarios) that require active decision-making and provide immediate feedback.
children.examples:
- Implementing branching scenarios where employees choose responses to potential social engineering phone calls.
- Using a serious game interface for policy training rather than static PDFs.
- Deploying dynamic, polymorphic warnings that change appearance to prevent habituation.
actionItems: Select interactive formats that prioritize decision-making over pure multimedia complexity.
Design interface elements that minimize cognitive load to avoid distracting from core security lessons.
Monitor interactive prompts and warnings for signs of user fatigue and reactance.
kpis: Threat identification speed and accuracy in interactive testing
User completion rate and feedback ratings for serious games
Click-through or ignore rate of interactive in-app warnings

children.name: Content Tailoring
children.description: Content tailoring involves customizing cybersecurity training materials and delivery methods to align with the specific roles, responsibilities, and risk profiles of distinct audience segments, rather than employing a generic “one-size-fits-all” strategy (Alshaikh et al., 2021, p. 14; Alyami et al., 2023, p. 65). This approach enhances effectiveness by ensuring that educational content is relevant to the learner's daily tasks and knowledge level, thereby increasing engagement and the likelihood of behavioral change (He & Zhang, 2019, p. 253; Hu et al., 2021, p. 756). Effective tailoring requires analyzing target groups to provide appropriate scenarios and messaging that resonate with their specific organizational context and culture (Kirova & Baumol, 2018, p. 27; Vasileiou & Furnell, 2019, p. 10).

children.name: Personal Life Relevance
children.description:
One of the most consistent findings in security awareness research is that employees do not ignore training because they are careless. They ignore it because it does not feel relevant to them. It reads as a corporate obligation, something that protects the organisation's assets at the cost of their time. Personal life relevance flips this dynamic by reframing security as something that protects the employee, their family, their finances, and their home, not just the company network (He & Zhang, 2019, p. 253).
The motivational logic is grounded in how people actually perceive risk. Abstract threats to organisational data are hard to feel personally invested in. The possibility that a weak password could expose a family member's bank account, or that a phishing link clicked at home could compromise a child's personal information, is immediate and tangible. Training that targets this personal risk perception consistently produces stronger engagement than compliance-framed alternatives (He & Zhang, 2019, p. 253). Sebescen (2015) makes this practical: show employees how their personal accounts could be compromised and how that affects their families, and the material becomes relevant in a way that no corporate policy document can match (p. 88).
There is also a reciprocity effect worth noting. When an organisation invests in helping employees stay safe at home, it signals that it cares about them as people rather than just as security risks to be managed. Employees tend to respond in kind, becoming more motivated to protect the organisation because the relationship feels mutual (He & Zhang, 2019, p. 253).
In practice, this suggests a specific sequencing approach. He and Zhang (2019) recommend starting with personal online safety topics, safe banking, social media privacy, talking to children about internet safety, before transitioning to workplace-specific content (p. 254). Engagement established through personally relevant material carries over into the professional context far more effectively than starting with corporate policy and hoping it lands. The fact that 84% of employees in one study applied work-based awareness training to reduce risks at home suggests this transfer works in both directions (Sabillon et al., 2019, p. 26).
The cognitive benefits reinforce the motivational ones. Personal relevance is not just a hook to get people into the training. It is a memory aid. Content that connects to someone's own life, their background, their habits, their family, is simply easier to recall than abstract guidance (Oldham & McAlpine, 2019, p. 116, 118). It also helps acclimatise employees to new security habits by anchoring unfamiliar behaviours to familiar personal contexts, which is one of the mechanisms NIST identifies for helping people incorporate new practices into existing behaviour patterns (Tsohou et al., 2008, p. 213).
For practitioners, the shift required here is less about content redesign and more about framing. The same underlying security lesson, delivered as a corporate requirement versus as something that protects your family, can produce very different levels of engagement.
children.tldr: Reframes cybersecurity training around protecting personal finances, family, and home networks, boosting motivation and leveraging reciprocity.
children.examples:
- Conducting a webinar for employees on how to secure home Wi-Fi and protect children's online privacy.
- Offering free personal password manager licenses to employees and their immediate families.
- Designing a learning module focusing on identifying scam calls targeting personal bank accounts.
actionItems: Integrate personal cybersecurity tips (e.g. home network defense, identity protection) into the onboarding training curriculum.
Provide resources or tools (e.g. password managers, security check-up checklists) for personal and family use.
Structure training schedules to begin with personal safety concepts before introducing corporate policy requirements.
kpis: Voluntary training completion and engagement rates for home-safety modules
Survey measurement of employee trust and perceived organizational care
Retention of security behaviors that transfer between home and office

children.name: Role based scenarios
children.description:
Generic security training has a fundamental credibility problem. When a warehouse operative and a finance director sit through the same module, neither feels like it was made for them, because it wasn't. Role-based scenarios solve this by grounding training in the actual work context of the learner, the threats they are likely to encounter, the decisions they are likely to face, and the access they actually hold (Vasileiou & Furnell, 2019, p. 136; Thamae, 2024, p. 132).
The relevance argument is straightforward. Training that reflects what someone does every day feels credible and actionable. Training that doesn't feels like a compliance exercise to be tolerated and forgotten (He & Zhang, 2019, p. 251). But role-based tailoring is not just about engagement. Different roles carry genuinely different risk profiles, and those differences matter. An employee authorised to transfer funds, a network administrator with elevated access, or an HR manager holding sensitive personnel data, each represents a higher-value target and requires more focused attention than the general workforce (Vasileiou & Furnell, 2019, p. 169; Dawson, 2019, p. 54).
In practice, role-based design requires thinking carefully about context. Barendse (2023) describes a case where training was split between office employees and factory workers, not as an aesthetic choice, but because the threat landscapes were genuinely different. Factory workers dealing with Operational Technology faced risks that had no relevance to a desk-based colleague, and vice versa. The same principle applies across any organisation with diverse functions.
Executives deserve particular attention here. They are frequently high-value targets, sometimes made more vulnerable by the preferential treatment they receive, such as being exempted from certain security controls. Role-specific scenarios for this group need to address both their personal exposure and the ways social engineers may impersonate them to manipulate others lower in the hierarchy (Vasileiou & Furnell, 2019, p. 166).
Scholl et al. (2017) offer a useful tiered framework for thinking about depth. General threat recognition is appropriate for all staff. Management and specialist roles warrant deeper content covering privileged access risks and the obligations that come with handling sensitive information (p. 435). This layered approach prevents the twin failure modes of overwhelming non-technical employees and boring technical ones with content that does not match their level.
The outcomes associated with role-based approaches are consistently positive. Customised training improves compliance, increases motivation to maintain awareness, and, perhaps most valuably, opens the door to a cultural shift. When security teams sit down with individual business units to discuss their specific risks, the conversation moves from enforcement to enablement, and that changes how employees relate to security altogether (Alshaikh et al., 2021, p. 11; Thamae, 2024, p. 132; Sabillon et al., 2019, p. 252).
children.tldr: Aligns security training scenarios with the specific daily workflows, technologies, risk exposure, and privileges of individual job roles.
children.examples:
- Designing tailored wire transfer fraud scenarios specifically for the finance team.
- Training warehouse or factory personnel on physical security and operational technology (OT) risks.
- Developing C-suite training scenarios that address executive spear-phishing and VIP-impersonation risks.
actionItems: Map organizational departments and roles to identify high-privilege and high-risk groups.
Design distinct, context-specific scenarios for each major group instead of a one-size-fits-all training module.
Integrate management-level risk ownership lessons for C-suite and department leads.
kpis: Reduction in click/compromise rate for role-specific phishing simulations
Training satisfaction and feedback scores segmented by target departments
Incident reporting rates for targeted attack vectors (e.g. BEC reported by finance)

children.name: Audience Segmentation
children.description:
Audience segmentation is the foundation that makes everything else in content tailoring actually work. Without it, even well-designed training gets delivered to the wrong people in the wrong way. The core argument in the literature is blunt: a single programme applied uniformly across an organisation is not a neutral choice, it is an active decision to make training less effective (Alshaikh et al., 2021, p. 7; Alyami et al., 2023, p. 65).
The reason segmentation matters goes beyond personalisation for its own sake. Different employee groups carry different risk profiles, hold different levels of access, respond to different communication styles, and bring different cultural assumptions to the table. A programme that does not account for these differences will routinely miss the people who need it most. Alshaikh et al. (2021) frame segmentation as a social marketing principle: by identifying groups that share common attitudes and readiness to change, organisations can apply targeted interventions where they will have the greatest impact (p. 8).
The most intuitive starting point is organisational role. The CATRAM model developed by Sabillon et al. (2019) offers a clean four-way split: executives and board members focused on governance; managers leading departmental initiatives; IT staff working with advanced technical content; and end users covering general workplace and personal safety (p. 241). This hierarchy is useful, but it should not be the only lens. Certain functional roles carry disproportionate risk regardless of seniority. Finance teams with wire transfer authority, HR staff with access to personnel data, and executive assistants who effectively hold the keys to senior leadership's systems, all warrant targeted attention that a hierarchy-only model tends to miss (Dawson, 2019, p. 1531; Vasileiou & Furnell, 2019, p. 166).
Cultural and environmental context adds another layer. Barendse (2023) documents a case where the same organisation needed entirely different content for office staff and factory workers, not just thematically but stylistically. The animated characters that worked well for desk-based employees were met with indifference on the factory floor, where workers dealing with Operational Technology responded better to on-site filmed content with an adventure theme (p. 47). National culture compounds this further: content that resonated strongly in Asia did not land the same way in Africa, and employees from collectivist cultures have been found to approach behaviours like password sharing very differently from those in more individualist contexts (Barendse, 2023, p. 47; Alyami et al., 2023, p. 66).
Finally, knowledge level and demographics shape what content is appropriate and how it should be pitched. Segmenting employees by existing proficiency prevents the twin failure modes of boring experts with basics and overwhelming novices with complexity (Hu et al., 2021, p. 755; Taherdoost, 2024, p. 1653). Research has also identified meaningful gender disparities in cybersecurity knowledge, pointing to the need for inclusive programme design that actively addresses those gaps rather than assuming a uniform baseline (Thamae, 2024, p. 129).
children.tldr: Divides the workforce into distinct subgroups based on role-specific risks, communication styles, culture, and proficiency levels to target training impact.
children.examples:
- Grouping employees into specialized pathways: executives, managers, technical IT staff, and general end users.
- Customizing content aesthetics and formats differently for office staff (e.g. digital modules) and factory workers (e.g. on-site adventure themes).
- Developing distinct baseline modules for non-technical novices versus advanced IT experts.
actionItems: Map key target audiences based on access privilege, risk profile, department, and cultural context.
Select tailored delivery formats and training aesthetics that resonate with each specific demographic.
Assess baseline employee knowledge to establish differentiated proficiency tiers for training paths.
kpis: Training engagement and completion rates segmented by department and demographic group
Incident reporting accuracy among high-value target groups (finance, HR, executive assistants)
Pre- and post-training assessment scores across distinct knowledge baselines

children.name: Executive Leadership
children.description: Executive leadership within the organizational perspective refers to the critical role of top management in defining governance, allocating resources, and demonstrating the commitment necessary to align information security strategies with business objectives (AlGhamdi et al., 2020, p. 2; Sabillon et al., 2017, p. 253). By adopting transformational leadership behaviors, such as articulating a security vision and promoting collective responsibility, executives significantly influence the development of a robust information security culture and enhance employee awareness (Flores & Ekstedt, 2016, p. 32; Thamae, 2024, p. 15). Furthermore, active engagement and buy-in from senior leadership set the tone for the organization, establishing the trust and motivation required to ensure employee compliance with security policies (Dawson, 2019, p. 112).

children.name: Visible Commitment
children.description: Visible commitment entails senior executives actively demonstrating their dedication to information security through observable behaviors, such as "leading by good example" in policy adherence, allocating necessary resources, and personally engaging in awareness activities (AlGhamdi et al., 2020, p. 7; Dawson, 2019, p. 112). By serving as role models and consistently communicating the strategic importance of cybersecurity, leaders signal its priority to the organization, which effectively motivates employee compliance and fosters a robust security culture (Thamae, 2024, p. 16). This approach transforms leadership support from a passive concept into a tangible influence, ensuring that management not only enforces rules but also legitimizes them through their own conduct (Tolah et al., 2021, p. 4).

children.name: Resource allocation
children.description: Resource allocation defines the strategic responsibility of executive leadership to provide the necessary financial, human, and technical assets required to support information security governance and awareness programs (AlGhamdi et al., 2020, p. 7; Thamae, 2024, p. 30). This process involves designating specific budgets, time, skilled personnel, and facilities to ensure that security policies and educational initiatives are effectively implemented and sustained (El-Haddadeh et al., 2012, p. 2; Tolah et al., 2021, p. 16). Effective allocation relies on top management's commitment to prioritizing security investments despite competing organizational demands and the challenges associated with measuring the return on investment for preventative measures (Assenza et al., 2020, p. 283; Dark, 2007, p. 12).

children.name: Policy Sponsorship
children.description: Policy sponsorship involves top management providing active support, commitment, and visible championship for information security policies to ensure they are recognized as strategic organizational priorities rather than merely technical issues (AlGhamdi et al., 2020, p. 2; Dawson, 2019, p. 112). This leadership function requires executives to act as role models to validate security initiatives, secure necessary resources, and signal the mandatory nature of compliance to the workforce (Alyami et al., 2023, p. 64; Hu et al., 2021, p. 5). Consequently, establishing executive sponsorship is critical for legitimizing security governance, fostering a strong security culture, and ensuring that policies are effectively implemented and enforced throughout the organization (El-Haddadeh et al., 2012, p. 4; Dawson, 2019, p. 130).

children.name: Organisational Culture
children.description: Organizational culture consists of the shared values, assumptions, and beliefs that guide employee behavior and decision-making, effectively defining "the way things are done" within an institution. In the context of information security, this culture is fundamental for embedding security practices into daily operations, ensuring that protective behaviors are internalized by employees rather than merely enforced through external policies. Consequently, a supportive organizational culture is critical for the success of security initiatives, as it shapes how employees perceive risks and prioritize their responsibilities in safeguarding information assets.

children.name: Security Ownership
children.description: Security ownership is defined as the extent to which employees view information security as their own responsibility and exhibit a willingness to actively support and enhance their security performance (Tolah et al., 2021, p. 6). This concept serves as a key reflection of security culture, manifesting when employees understand the necessity of information protection and the potential risks resulting from their actions (Tolah et al., 2021, p. 6). Fostering this sense of ownership helps transform employees from passive subjects into active participants who internalize security duties as part of their daily roles (Thamae, 2024, p. 30).

children.name: Psychological Safety
children.description: Psychological safety within an organizational security culture is characterized by an open and communicative environment where employees feel empowered to discuss security issues and report incidents without fear of repercussions or scrutiny (Barendse, 2023, p. 23; Oldham & McAlpine, 2019, p. 2). This approach avoids creating a "shaming culture," thereby encouraging employees to proactively report errors, such as clicking on phishing links, rather than concealing them due to anxiety or fear of punishment (Barendse, 2023, p. 86). Establishing this level of trust and transparency is critical for fostering ethical behavior and reducing the likelihood of insider threats (Thamae, 2024, p. 16).

children.name: Social Norms
children.description: Social norms, often conceptualized as subjective norms or normative beliefs, refer to the perceived social pressure and expectations from peers, supervisors, and the organization to perform specific security behaviors (Ali et al., 2021, p. 4; Pham, 2017, p. 7). Within the context of organizational culture, these norms represent shared values and tacit assumptions that guide employee conduct, often influencing behavior more effectively than formal written policies (Sutton & Tompson, 2025, p. 3). Consequently, when employees perceive that their social circle or influential organizational members expect compliance, they are more likely to internalize these standards and adopt secure behaviors (Bulgurcu et al., 2010, p. 528).

children.name: Policy Framework
children.description: From an organizational perspective, a policy framework serves as a comprehensive structure that aligns information security policies with business objectives to ensure the protection of assets and adherence to regulatory requirements (AlGhamdi et al., 2020, p. 2). This framework encompasses the development of clear rules, roles, and procedures—often grounded in established standards such as NIST or ISO—to guide employee behavior and foster a security-conscious culture (Ali et al., 2021, p. 2; Sabillon et al., 2017, p. 253). Furthermore, it integrates governance mechanisms to monitor compliance and manage risks, ensuring that security measures are effectively embedded within the organization's daily operations and strategic vision (Dawson, 2019, p. 2; Thamae, 2024, p. 35).

children.name: Role Specific Ownership
children.description: Role Specific Ownership entails the explicit definition and assignment of security responsibilities and risk ownership to distinct administrative units and job functions, avoiding a generic "one-size-fits-all" approach to compliance (AlGhamdi et al., 2020, p. 7; Vasileiou & Furnell, 2019, p. 10). This framework component ensures that individuals with higher degrees of access and authority, such as executives or IT staff, are held accountable for the specific assets and risks inherent to their positions (Vasileiou & Furnell, 2019, pp. 33-34). Ultimately, establishing these specific roles within policy fosters a sense of personal obligation and aligns employee behavior with the organization's security objectives (Tolah et al., 2021, p. 5; AlGhamdi et al., 2020, p. 30).

children.name: User Centric Design
children.description: User-centric design within an organizational context prioritizes understanding employees' specific work environments, skills, and constraints to create security solutions and training that are tailored to their needs rather than relying on generic compliance mandates (Nurse et al., 2025, p. 93; Taherdoost, 2024, p. 10). This approach utilizes collaborative feedback to identify the root causes of insecure behavior, enabling the development of accessible, user-friendly interventions that foster self-efficacy and intrinsic motivation (Nurse et al., 2025, p. 93). By centering the human element, organizations can ensure that security measures are relevant and accommodated to varying levels of technical proficiency, ultimately integrating security into the organizational culture more effectively (Nurse et al., 2025, p. 93; Taherdoost, 2024, p. 10).

children.name: Accessibility
children.description: In the context of organizational user-centric design, accessibility entails creating training programs that accommodate diverse learner needs by removing barriers related to visual, audio, or motor impairments to ensure equal access and opportunity for all employees (Oldham & McAlpine, 2019). This approach requires adherence to standards such as the Web Content Accessibility Guidelines (WCAG) to ensure compatibility with assistive technologies like screen readers, thereby enhancing the quality and retention of learning materials for the entire workforce (Oldham & McAlpine, 2019). Additionally, user-centric accessibility addresses varying levels of technical proficiency by utilizing adaptive learning paths and micro-learning modules to ensure users can effectively engage with the content regardless of their technical background (Taherdoost, 2024, p. 8, p. 12). **Sources:** Oldham, J., & McAlpine, H. (2019). Techniques and Tools for Trainers and Practitioners. In *Cybersecurity Education for Awareness and Compliance*. IGI Global. Taherdoost, H. (2024). Towards an Innovative Model for Cybersecurity Awareness Training. *Information*, *15*(9), 512.

children.name: Evaluation Methods
children.description: Evaluation methods in the design of security education, training, and awareness (SETA) programs are systematic procedures—often categorized as formative, summative, and confirmative—used to assess whether interventions successfully address performance gaps and influence employee behavior (Dark, 2007; Alshaikh et al., 2021). These methodologies utilize a combination of qualitative techniques, such as user feedback and observation, and quantitative metrics, including standardized surveys and simulated phishing exercises, to measure the acquisition of knowledge, skills, and security attitudes (Manifavas et al., 2014, p. 260; Scholl et al., 2017). Integrating these evaluations directly into the design lifecycle provides a feedback loop for continuous program improvement, allowing organizations to justify resources and adapt strategies to the specific security culture (Rantos et al., 2012; Griffin, 2011).

children.name: Independent Observations
children.description:
Independent observations involve the monitoring of employee behavior regarding information security policies without alerting the subjects, thereby capturing "true" behavior and avoiding the Hawthorne effect (where behavior changes because it is being observed). Independent observations, or "silent monitoring," involve discretion (Rantos et al., 2012, p. 336) to prevent temporary behavioral changes by employees. A primary application of this method is the physical inspection of workspaces. "Conducting walkabouts", as described by Assenza et al., 2020, serve to determine if staff respect basic security rules during the "everyday routine" (p. 277).
Role in the Evaluation Framework
Independent observations are positioned as a critical component of a holistic evaluation strategy, complementing other methods like surveys and automated metrics. While independent observations are often classified as qualitative techniques used to capture "employees' sensation regarding awareness," they can be quantified to provide benchmarks (Manifavas et al., 2014, p. 260). Assenza et al. (2020) argue that while monitoring security incidents provides a differential indicator, it is reactive. In contrast, observing daily operations through walkabouts offers a measure of the organization's security posture regarding "standard operations" rather than just emergencies (p. 277).
Advantages and Limitations
The sources highlight trade-offs regarding reliability, cost, and organizational culture when using independent observations. Assenza et al. (2020) classify walkabouts and security incident monitoring as having high reliability because they generate unbiased results regarding actual behavior (p. 282). However, Assenza et al. (2020) also note that tools investigating behavior (like walkabouts) are often "more time and resource consuming" compared to methods measuring knowledge or attitude (p. 282).Yet, Scholl et al. (2017) warn that while hidden observations produce realistic results, they may lead to a "more paranoid atmosphere when conducted multiple times" (p. 433). To mitigate legal and cultural risks, they suggest that observations can sometimes be performed in controlled environments, such as role-playing, though this trades some realism for organizational harmony (Scholl et al., 2017, p. 433).
children.tldr: Monitors employee behavior silently and without prior notice to assess everyday policy adherence (e.g. clean desk checks) without triggering the Hawthorne effect.
children.examples:
- Conducting after-hours physical office sweeps ("walkabouts") to check for unlocked workstations or post-it notes containing passwords.
- Reviewing system logs to verify if employees log out of sensitive applications before leaving their desks, without notifying the employees of the audit.
- Observing employee reactions and compliance patterns during standard operations to establish a behavioral baseline, rather than only relying on post-incident audits.
actionItems: Establish clear protocols and guidelines for conducting discreet security "walkabouts" and workspace checks.
Quantify qualitative observations (e.g., counting instances of unlocked screens) to establish behavioral baselines.
Mitigate cultural friction and paranoid atmosphere by occasionally running checks in controlled environments like role-playing or training simulations.
kpis: Percentage of workstations left unlocked or unattended during random physical audits
Rate of clean desk policy compliance (e.g., passwords on sticky notes, sensitive documents left in the open)
Year-over-year reduction in standard-operation security policy infractions discovered via silent audits

children.name: Phishing Simulations
children.description:
Phishing simulations occupy a unique position in the evaluation toolkit: they are the closest thing to a real test of whether security awareness training is actually working. Unlike surveys or knowledge checks, which measure what employees say they know, simulations measure what employees do when they think no one is watching. That distinction matters enormously (Scholl et al., 2017, p. 433).

The first practical use of simulations is establishing a baseline. Before any training intervention, running a simulation across a range of phishing templates gives organisations a honest picture of where they stand, click rates, reporting rates, and which groups are most vulnerable. Yeoh et al. (2021) describe exactly this approach, using 14 templates to establish an initial click rate of 12% and a reporting rate of 8.3% before training began (p. 10). Without that baseline, it is impossible to demonstrate whether anything has improved.

A point worth emphasising: the reporting rate matters as much as the click rate, arguably more. A falling click rate tells you employees are getting better at avoiding mistakes. A rising reporting rate tells you they are actively participating in organisational defence. After six cycles of simulation and embedded training, Yeoh et al. (2021) recorded a reporting rate that climbed from 8% to 35%, which represents a meaningful cultural shift, not just individual improvement (p. 11).

For simulations to produce valid results, realism is non-negotiable. Two factors are particularly easy to get wrong. First, contextual relevance: employees are actually less likely to spot suspicious cues when a phishing email closely mirrors their normal work context, such as a convincing HR message arriving during a busy period (Jager & Eckhardt, 2021, p. 5). This means varying the context of simulations is essential to getting an accurate read on vulnerability. Second, time pressure: real phishing attacks exploit busy, distracted people. Simulations that do not replicate this pressure are testing a different, easier version of the problem (Wen et al., 2019, p. 4).

The most effective simulation programmes do not just evaluate, they correct. Pairing a simulation with immediate embedded training, a short video or explanatory page triggered the moment someone clicks, turns the evaluation itself into a teachable moment. Yeoh et al. (2021) frame this through operant conditioning: correct behaviour gets reinforced with positive feedback, while a mistake triggers training rather than punishment (p. 7). The correction lands at precisely the moment the employee is most receptive to it (Oldham & McAlpine, 2019, p. 119).

Two limitations deserve attention. Repeated unannounced simulations can erode trust and create a surveillance atmosphere that damages morale and, in some contexts, raises legal questions (Scholl et al., 2017, p. 433). Predictability is the other risk: if employees begin to recognise simulation patterns, the exercise loses its validity as a behavioural measure (Reeves et al., 2021, p. 6). Varying timing, format, and complexity across cycles is essential to keeping results meaningful.
children.tldr: Measures actual behavior by deploying realistic, simulated phishing emails to track click and active reporting rates under real-world conditions.
children.examples:
- Launching an initial baseline simulation using various templates to record pre-training click and reporting rates.
- Sending targeted simulated phishing emails designed to exploit contextual relevance (e.g. urgent HR messages during busy seasons) or time pressure.
- Triggering immediate educational feedback or modules (such as a 1-minute video) the moment an employee clicks a simulated link.
actionItems: Run multi-template phishing simulations before training to establish honest behavioral baselines.
Design simulations with realistic context, time pressure, and variable complexity across cycles.
Pair simulated failures with immediate, constructive educational feedback rather than punitive measures.
kpis: Simulation click rate (failure rate) across different departments and complexity tiers
Simulation active reporting rate (success rate) to gauge active organizational defense participation
Time taken by employees to report simulated phishing emails to the security operations center (SOC)

children.name: Validated questionnaires
children.description:
Most security awareness programmes track completion rates. Fewer track whether the training actually changed anything. Validated questionnaires are one of the more rigorous tools available for closing that gap, offering a standardised way to measure not just what employees know, but how they think about security and how they report behaving (Parsons et al., 2017, p. 41; Assenza et al., 2020, p. 266).
The distinction between a validated questionnaire and a standard feedback form is not cosmetic. Validated instruments are built through a structured development process, pilot testing for clarity and length, factor analysis to confirm that questions actually measure the constructs they are supposed to measure, and iterative refinement to remove items that do not perform reliably (Bulgurcu et al., 2010, p. 534; Sutton & Tompson, 2025, p. 104110). The result is a tool that produces consistent, interpretable results over time, rather than a snapshot of how employees felt on a given Tuesday.
The most well-known example is the Human Aspects of Information Security Questionnaire (HAIS-Q), designed to measure knowledge, attitude, and behaviour across areas like password management, email use, and incident reporting. Crucially, it has demonstrated predictive validity: employees who score well on the HAIS-Q also perform better in live phishing experiments, which means the questionnaire is not just capturing self-perception but something closer to actual susceptibility (Parsons et al., 2017, p. 43; Moustafa et al., 2021, p. 5). For practitioners, that predictive capability makes validated questionnaires a genuinely useful diagnostic tool, not just a post-training formality.
That said, the limitations are real and worth building into any evaluation design. The most significant is social desirability bias: employees tend to give answers that reflect how they think they should behave rather than how they actually behave, particularly on sensitive topics like incident reporting. Parsons et al. (2017) found this effect most pronounced around reporting behaviour, likely shaped by cultural reluctance to be seen calling out colleagues (p. 43). This is not a flaw unique to any particular instrument; it is a structural limitation of self-report methods generally.
The knowing-doing gap compounds this. An employee can score highly on a knowledge questionnaire and still click a phishing link under time pressure (Flores & Ekstedt, 2016, p. 26). Questionnaires measure what people know and believe; simulations and observations measure what people do. Neither is sufficient alone, and the most robust evaluation frameworks combine both (Assenza et al., 2020, p. 266; Manifavas et al., 2014, p. 260).
